questionText: The lifetimes, $X$ hours, of a certain brand of light bulb are modelled by $X \sim N(1200, 80^2)$. (a) Find $P(X > 1300)$. (b) A carton contains $400$ of these bulbs. Estimate how many of them last more than $1300$ hours. Give probabilities to $4$ significant figures.

steps.description: State the distribution and the probability required
steps.explanation: The lifetimes are normal with mean $\mu = 1200$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 80$ (remember the $80^2$ in $N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ is the variance, so $\sigma = 80$). Part (a) asks for the chance a single bulb outlasts $1300$ hours.
To use the standard normal we measure how many standard deviations $1300$ lies above the mean. Subtracting the mean and dividing by $\sigma$ turns the lifetime into a $z$-value of $1.25$.

steps.description: Rewrite the probability in terms of Z
steps.explanation: Because standardising preserves the inequality, the chance $X$ exceeds $1300$ is exactly the chance the standard normal $Z$ exceeds $1.25$.

steps.description: Convert the upper tail to a cumulative probability
steps.explanation: The cumulative function $\Phi(z)$ gives the area to the left of $z$. The area to the right is whatever is left over from the total of $1$, so we subtract $\Phi(1.25)$ from $1$.
Reading the standard normal CDF at $z = 1.25$ (from a calculator) gives $0.8944$: about $89\%$ of bulbs fail before $1300$ hours.
Subtracting from $1$ leaves the upper-tail probability. So roughly $10.6\%$ of bulbs last longer than $1300$ hours.

steps.description: Set up the expected number for part (b)
steps.explanation: Each of the $400$ bulbs independently has probability $0.1056$ of lasting past $1300$ hours, so the expected count is the number of bulbs multiplied by that probability.
Multiplying $400$ by $0.1056$ gives $42.26$, the average number of long-lasting bulbs we would expect in a carton of this size.

steps.description: Round to a whole number of bulbs
steps.explanation: Since we are counting whole bulbs, we round to $42$. This is a sensible estimate: only about one in ten bulbs is a long-lasting one.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $P(X > 1300) = 0.1056$ (4 s.f.); about $42$ of the $400$ bulbs are expected to last more than $1300$ hours.
